The biggest problem here is that most agencies--like MMDA--will implement it in a different way.

If an agency opts for a compressed work week (4 days work, 10 hours of per day), isn't that going to result in the same amount of energy consumed as a 5-day, 8-hour work week?

If the objective of the 4 day workweek is to reduce the transportation expenses of employees. I believe such objective  can be attained.

However, by stretching the working hours, productivity is affected. A human comprehension is effective only on the 1st 45 minutes. It plateaus for the rest of the day. In an 8 hour workday, an 80% daily productivity is the ideal number. But hardly consistently achievable. What more of a 10 hour workday.

Productivity has never been about long hours. 

Why not retain a 4 day 8 hour workday and a work from home on the 5th?

Apart from various types of allowances there are also lots of other benefits and bonuses including stocks, 13th month pay, bonuses, incentives, etc. In the end at times, the basic salary may be a small portion of the overall remuneration package. If this practice has existed for many years and a lawmaker sincerely believes it is wrong, the logical and responsible first step is correction, not spectacle. Congress is not just a place to expose problems. It is the institution empowered to fix them. Publicly insinuating wrongdoing without immediately advancing concrete reforms weakens the institution more than it strengthens accountability.

It is also important to separate two issues that are being blurred. First, large allocations of public funds are not automatically corrupt. Second, the absence of transparency and enforceable liquidation standards is where abuse becomes possible. The problem is not only the amount. The problem is opacity. If these funds were clearly itemized, strictly liquidated, published in full and made accessible to taxpayers in real time, the risk of abuse would be significantly reduced. That is a policy failure that Congress itself has the authority to correct.

If a lawmaker believes break allowances or lump sum MOOE releases are being used improperly, the proper response is to file measures that mandate disclosure, tighten liquidation requirements, remove certification only liquidations and impose penalties for misuse. Those are concrete actions. 

Posting allegations online without a parallel legislative proposal turns a governance issue into a narrative battle rather than an institutional reform effort.

There is also a question of responsibility. Maligning the House as a whole while remaining part of it, without offering a clear corrective mechanism, erodes public trust without giving the public a solution.

Transparency should be institutionalized, not personalized. Reform should be structural, not reactive. If the system is flawed, then fix the system using the tools of lawmaking, not implication.

In the end, lawmakers are elected to strengthen the bureaucracy, not to weaken it through selective exposure. Accountability that leads to reform is valuable. Outrage without a roadmap is not. If this practice is truly problematic, the remedy lies in legislation, clear rules, open books, and sustained oversight, not in politicized disclosure that leaves the structure exactly as it was.
